verses.text: Job answers the LORD: 'I know that thou canst do every thing,' confessing he had spoken of what he did not understand, things too wonderful for him. He bows before God's wisdom. The end of all his questioning is humble submission to a God too great to fathom.

Job answers the LORD: 'I know that thou canst do every thing,' confessing he had spoken of what he did not understand, things too wonderful for him. He bows before God's wisdom. The end of all his questioning is humble submission to a God too great to fathom.

Job answers the LORD: 'I know that thou canst do every thing,' confessing he had spoken of what he did not understand, things too wonderful for him. He bows before God's wisdom. The end of all his questioning is humble submission to a God too great to fathom.

'I have heard of thee by the hearing of the ear: but now mine eye seeth thee,' Job says; 'wherefore I abhor myself, and repent in dust and ashes.' A new sight of God breaks his heart. To truly see God is to be humbled, and to find peace at last in repentance.

'I have heard of thee by the hearing of the ear: but now mine eye seeth thee,' Job says; 'wherefore I abhor myself, and repent in dust and ashes.' A new sight of God breaks his heart. To truly see God is to be humbled, and to find peace at last in repentance.

'I have heard of thee by the hearing of the ear: but now mine eye seeth thee,' Job says; 'wherefore I abhor myself, and repent in dust and ashes.' A new sight of God breaks his heart. To truly see God is to be humbled, and to find peace at last in repentance.

God rebukes the three friends, for they 'have not spoken of me the thing that is right, as my servant Job,' and bids them bring offerings while Job prays for them. The wronged man becomes their intercessor. God vindicates the sufferer and honours him to plead for his accusers.

God rebukes the three friends, for they 'have not spoken of me the thing that is right, as my servant Job,' and bids them bring offerings while Job prays for them. The wronged man becomes their intercessor. God vindicates the sufferer and honours him to plead for his accusers.

God rebukes the three friends, for they 'have not spoken of me the thing that is right, as my servant Job,' and bids them bring offerings while Job prays for them. The wronged man becomes their intercessor. God vindicates the sufferer and honours him to plead for his accusers.

When Job prayed for his friends, the LORD turned his captivity and gave him twice as much as before, and his kindred came to comfort him. Restoration followed forgiveness. God blesses the heart that lets go of its grievance and prays for those who wronged it.

When Job prayed for his friends, the LORD turned his captivity and gave him twice as much as before, and his kindred came to comfort him. Restoration followed forgiveness. God blesses the heart that lets go of its grievance and prays for those who wronged it.

The LORD blessed Job's latter end more than his beginning, with double the flocks and seven sons and three daughters. His losses were more than restored. The God who wounds for a season can heal beyond all that was lost.

The LORD blessed Job's latter end more than his beginning, with double the flocks and seven sons and three daughters. His losses were more than restored. The God who wounds for a season can heal beyond all that was lost.

His daughters were the fairest in the land, and he gave them an inheritance among their brothers. Even this detail shows his renewed honour and tenderness. God's restored mercies overflow into the next generation.

His daughters were the fairest in the land, and he gave them an inheritance among their brothers. Even this detail shows his renewed honour and tenderness. God's restored mercies overflow into the next generation.

Job lived a hundred and forty years, saw four generations of his children, and 'died, being old and full of days.' The story of suffering ends in peace. God brings His tried servant through the storm to a settled, blessed close.

Job lived a hundred and forty years, saw four generations of his children, and 'died, being old and full of days.' The story of suffering ends in peace. God brings His tried servant through the storm to a settled, blessed close.
